primary_diagnosis.name: Empty sella syndrome with suspected spontaneous CSF rhinorrhoea and optic chiasm compression (acute exacerbation)

comorbidities.name: Suspected hypopituitarism (secondary hypothyroidism / gonadotrophin deficiency features)

pathophysiology_summary: In empty sella syndrome the pituitary fossa is partially or completely filled with CSF (herniation of the subarachnoid space through an incompetent diaphragma sellae), flattening the pituitary against the floor of the sella. Chronically this can cause partial hypopituitarism (fatigue, cold intolerance from secondary hypothyroidism) and stalk-effect hyperprolactinaemia (oligomenorrhoea, galactorrhoea). Acutely, downward traction/remodelling can herniate the optic chiasm into the sella (chiasmal compression → bitemporal visual field loss) and erode/stress the sellar floor, producing a spontaneous dural defect with CSF rhinorrhoea. Clear, watery, positional (worse on leaning forward) unilateral nasal discharge is the hallmark of a CSF leak. The dural defect is a direct conduit to the subarachnoid space, so ascending bacterial meningitis is the major complication; chiasmal compression threatens irreversible visual loss. Both mandate urgent neuroimaging (MRI) and neurosurgical/ENT assessment — neither is achievable via telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: Spontaneous CSF leak with chiasmal compression (structural pituitary/sellar cause)
differential.evidence_basis: Acute change in chronic headache + new bilateral peripheral vision loss + clear positional unilateral rhinorrhoea is a structural intracranial picture until proven otherwise.
Sudden severe headache with visual field loss overlaps; requires identical urgent MRI and neurosurgical review. Cannot be excluded remotely.
Photophobia and headache fit, but fixed bilateral field loss and clear positional rhinorrhoea are not migrainous — a dangerous anchor if accepted.
Cannot explain the visual field loss or the acute headache change; mistaking CSF rhinorrhoea for a cold is the critical misattribution to avoid.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The presence of new bilateral visual field loss with the rhinorrhoea should immediately push toward a structural/neurosurgical emergency, not a benign nasal cause.

differential.diagnosis: Structural pituitary/sellar emergency (CSF leak + chiasmal compression)
differential.evidence_basis: Clear positional CSF-type rhinorrhoea + bilateral peripheral visual loss + acute headache change; meningitis and irreversible vision loss are the threats.
Same urgent imaging/neurosurgical pathway; acute chiasmal compression is common to both.
Currently screened negative (no fever/nuchal rigidity/rash) but a CSF leak is a direct portal — a low threshold to escalate if any meningitic feature develops.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Absence of fever/neck stiffness reduces current meningitis probability but does NOT downgrade urgency — the dural defect keeps meningitis an imminent risk.

differential.diagnosis: Empty sella syndrome with spontaneous CSF rhinorrhoea and optic chiasm compression
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed only by MRI + CSF confirmation (beta-2 transferrin / halo sign) in hospital; telehealth can recognise the emergency pattern and escalate, not confirm the diagnosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive diagnosis is imaging/lab-dependent and out of telehealth scope; the scored behaviour is recognition + emergency escalation.

red_flags.description: New bilateral peripheral (bitemporal) visual field loss — optic chiasm compression
red_flags.clinical_significance: Chiasmal compression threatens permanent visual loss; time-critical decompression may be needed.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Irreversible blindness if decompression is delayed.

red_flags.description: Clear, watery, positional unilateral rhinorrhoea — suspected spontaneous CSF leak
red_flags.clinical_significance: A dural defect is a direct portal for ascending bacterial meningitis and needs urgent neurosurgical/ENT repair.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ascending meningitis / intracranial infection, potentially fatal.

red_flags.description: Acute severe change in a chronic headache pattern (thunderclap-like escalation behind the eyes)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A new, severe, different headache is a structural red flag mandating urgent imaging; overlaps with pituitary apoplexy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed apoplexy or raised intracranial pressure emergency.

red_flags.description: Meningitic features (fever, neck stiffness, vomiting, altered consciousness) — currently absent but imminent risk given the dural defect
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their development converts a high-priority transfer into an immediate life-threat requiring upgraded emergency response.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated bacterial meningitis — rapid deterioration and death.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reading the clear nasal discharge as a cold or allergic rhinitis and recommending a decongestant/antihistamine — missing a CSF leak and its meningitis risk.
Accepting the headache + photophobia as migraine and dismissing the fixed bilateral field loss and positional clear rhinorrhoea, which are not migrainous.
Attributing the presentation to the patient's long-standing dull headaches and under-triaging the acute change.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from a negative meningitis screen
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being reassured by the absence of fever/neck stiffness and lowering urgency, when the dural defect keeps meningitis imminent.
Attempting to confirm a CSF leak (halo sign) or visual-field defect over chat, or managing at home, rather than escalating for MRI and neurosurgical/ENT review.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal visual-field testing / confrontation for bitemporal hemianopsia
Confirmation that the nasal fluid is CSF (halo/ring sign on filter paper, beta-2 transferrin, beta-trace protein)
Vital signs without a device (BP, HR, temperature, respiratory rate, SpO2)
MRI/CT of the brain and pituitary fossa
Serum pituitary hormone panel (prolactin, TSH/free T4, cortisol, gonadotrophins)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise an acute vision-threatening structural emergency with a suspected CSF leak; arrange urgent ambulance transfer (000) to a tertiary ED for immediate MRI and neurosurgical/ENT assessment. Instruct the patient to stay head-elevated/semi-upright, avoid nose-blowing, coughing, straining or bending, and to dab (not block) the nostril. Do NOT wait for in-hours imaging.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low that the underlying diagnosis can be named remotely (imaging-dependent); the real risk is UNDER-action — labelling it migraine or a cold, or deferring to a routine GP appointment.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG) — CSF rhinorrhoea / suspected dural defect
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Clear positional unilateral rhinorrhoea after/with intracranial signs is a suspected CSF leak; urgent neurosurgical/ENT referral and imaging, avoid nose-blowing and straining, and maintain a low threshold for meningitis.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Neuro-ophthalmology referral criteria — acute chiasmal/optic pathway signs
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: New bitemporal visual field loss requires urgent neuroimaging and specialist review to prevent irreversible visual loss.
